author: @MADMOVIESINC
text:
How do I feel about it? I fraking hate it. This is also basically the opposite of the beginnings of Disney. I’m down with using it as a tool for mundane tasks, but I doubt that a corporation will be able to maintain balance of that with the creative side. I feel like they will push it too far. They can’t help themselves. Making complete projects with these tools will not look quality. It also takes the heart out of projects. 

It’s great for the small groups of people or individuals, as we see on YouTube, because they don’t have the assets and backing to do proper films. 

For Disney to say that they want to just create completely generated films is just lazy and sad. 

I really hope that the art of boycotting comes back into fashion. If people stop paying for this crap, then it won’t be profitable and they will abandon it. 

The only way these people will learn is if you hurt their pocket book. They are too shortsighted and don’t care about the art.

author: @TheBingBang77
text: 1. In a film class in college, I remember rotoscoping lightsabers in after effects for a Fan Film we made (Star Wars Sacrifices though not sure if it's on YouTube still or not). It was arduous and tedious. 2. I'll reiterate my opinion about CG when it was new and upcoming. It's a tool to help enhance a story on visual media, not BE the story. I think both Peter Jackson and George Lucas said something to that effect. So I feel the same about AI. As long as it's a tool used ethically to aid/enhance the visual story to supplement and not replace, I'm all in.

replies.author: @TheVisualDigitalArts
replies.text:
The problem with current AI tools is they don’t help or speed up the process the tools are the process I’m always looking for tools to help in my art process like Retoplogy and UV work but all the AI tools that I could find always takes away my ability to to have control in my work with AI you always have to say “good enough”.

There are som AI mocap tools that could help but they all use the tokens payment model which is even worse than the subscription model.

replies.author: @christophmahler
replies.text:
@TheVisualDigitalArts 
"(...) with AI you always have to say “good enough”" 

Noticed that as well. 
The model is limited to the data, it was trained upon, requiring local training for mximizing control over the outcome. 

Then there is 'Agent Poisening' of the entire model that won't be discovered until the secret prompt commands are given... _Trojan Malware_ by definition.

author: @JTHarrisHF
text:
BRING COSTS DOWN!!

FACT: There is no way a company can survive if it pays 250 million to make 8 - 35min episodes of the Acolyte. Paramount only spent about 100 million in season 3 of Picard yet it made 10 - 1 hr episodes of Picard while using all the original cast! 
That’s more than double the amount of content while paying less than half the price!

They’re already getting blown away at that level. Imagine if they tried to compete against AI!

They 100% HAVE TO embrace it at the production level, but at the creative level, it should not go further than just an extra opinion. 

Imagine a writer‘s room, but now you also have the opinion of AI, throwing its ideas into the hat. That is 100% doable but in the end, it still needs to be approved by a human.

If they get lazy, let AI write the script thinking that who cares it’s all the same to them…..they’re TOAST.

author: @Apoch86
text:
I work in post production (compositing) vfx for film... we have been using AI for roto, and sometimes for stuff like face replacements, or paint... There have been some uses for doing one off shots for things like birds and whatnot as well..

Ultimately this stuff is helpful "when it works"... Half the time, you can try to use it, it'll fail, and if you try to fix it, it'll still likely fail, so it actually wastes money, where if we'd just done it the "hard" way in the first place, it'd be done properly...

AI is a tool, not a comprehensive solution.

author: @KryyssTV
text:
Disney doesn't realise that the AI datacentres they need for generative video already run at a significant loss. So much so that investor analysts are recommending that people not put their money into generative AI media companies.

Once those public access datacentres close down and Disney needs to own their own for cinema scale production, they'll realise it was cheaper to just use regular post-production pipelines.

replies.author: @Encom-T1
replies.text: Maybe, but AI is getting easier to run on slower cheaper hardware. Also, SpaceX is going to do AI datacenters in space. Solar powered 24/7

replies.author: @KryyssTV
replies.text:
​@Encom-T1 The world consumes  20 terawatts of power each day. The datacentres for the internet use 0.24TW while AI datacentres use 0.06TW (60 Gigawatts). Solar energy won't even begin to come close to that.

One nuclear power plant produces 1GW of energy per day meaning that even now you'd need 60 nuclear reactors orbitting earth if those data centres were in space.

author: @Opnn8d1
text:
If the AI that Disney uses in its internal process is trained exclusively on the audio/visual art they own and retain usage rights over based on previous resolution of contractual obligations between them and the artists, then I have absolutely no problem with it being used. In terms of generative AI voices and/or likenesses of actors involved in those IPs, there is the concern over compensation, direct or residual. And they need to address that concern pro-actively and in favor of the actors. They cannot just treat this as a "let's save money by replacing people with AI." 

I wonder what Harrison Ford would think if Disney decided to release a series of short productions featuring Indiana Jones, where generative AI uses his likeness and voice, but he gets neither credit nor compensation for whatever new material is presented. Mark Hammil is on record as having said that perhaps a conversation needs to be had about using his voice and likeness. We know that James Earl Jones granted Disney the rights to use his voice as Darth Vader in perpetuity.

Ultimately, it comes down to the precise wording of contracts between Disney and the actors concerning usage rights of material produced involving both likeness and voice. If open to broad enough interpretation, Disney may be legally entitled to use any audio or video footage in any way they see fit, which by literal definition would include generative AI, even though the medium did not exist when the contracts were written and signed in good faith. Legal loopholes are still legal and there may not be a case to be had against them. However, a legal concession that could be mandated is that going forward, all contracts must specifically include consent for voice/likeness to be used in generative AI. 

Now a way they can do recognizable characters like Indiana Jones without worrying about any compensation to Harrison Ford is if they use a person who bears a striking resemblance to Ford, and a person whose voice sounds very close, then they can honestly say that neither Ford's voice nor likeness was used. Same thing goes for every single other character they may want to "immortalize" in AI. The CHARACTER would be accurately presented, but no royalties/compensation would be owed to the original actors. 

But to keep the productions themselves from being slop, they must aim for complete consistency which will require heavy use of careful prompting and reference shots. And the writing needs to be done by people who fully understand the characters and world the stories involve. Generative AI videos being produced by average people now are way better than they were just a year ago. And the process continues to improve with each iteration of the software being used. So yeah. As long as they approach it in a way that doesn't screw over the original actors, the storytelling is solid, and the visual presentation is top notch and consistent, I can get behind it. Otherwise, I won't watch it, or even subscribe to Disney+.

author: @1987chrisw
text: I think it's a good thing. Part of the reason why SW animation is better than the live action stuff is because there isn't a billion dollars on the line if it fails to find an audience. The lower cost project does not have to try and appeal to absolutely everyone. That frees up writers to take more risks and make more interesting stories. Shows like Andor struggle to be profitable, if you can replace lots of the expensive VFX with AI then you can make more targeted shows like Andor that natural have a more narrow audience.

author: @JustinBeller
text: All of this investment in AI means nothing unless you can tell a good story.

author: @michaeltunnicliffe4935
text: I worry about AI replacing humans. But I also value it as a tool. I support AI if it is only to assist with appearances and CGI etc. After all. Look at Tarkin in Rogue One. That's clearly not Peter Cushing. CGI was used to make Guy Henry look like Tarkin. But it was still Henry acting the part and speaking the lines (with a very good impression.) what's the difference between CGI and AI when used in this function? Where I would draw a line is of they outright used AI to negate needing an actor at all. Or of they used AI to create a soundtrack. I mean what is Star Wars without the amazing compositions of John Williams. AI has the potential to put all authors, musicians, artists and actors out of work. And I don't want to live in a world that devoid of humanity. But making Mark Hamill look younger or adding in a quick CGI cameo, I can live with

author: @TheVortexPrime
text: I reckon they should dial it to 11 with AI, get the story written by AI, do all the CGI with AI, just go all out. A company that wants to make a profit or is passionate about the franchise would give the fans what they want, this is not Disney's goal. Their goal seems to be to lay waste to themselves and I'm not just talking star wars, I'm talking marvel and snow white, etc, they keep screwing up across the board. I say if you're set on doing something, do it right, so go full out with AI, run the company into the ground and then maybe star wars can be sold to someone who cares about the fans and is competent in making good decisions.

text: my opinion - I have no issue with AI if used right as we have seen here before and on other star wars content creators.. BUT if disney decide to.. take this new neat idea 'they discovered' COFF COFF! and milk it and do a piss poor job then i do object... this tech is a way to prolong the classic eras of star wars.. deliver tales we want to see from legends and beyond and a way to fill the void to establish a TRUE sequel trilogy.. If disney takes the ideas and work done by others BETTER and flubs it in the name of profit then i couldnt care less.. but if they give the devs and content creators already pulling off these epic scale pieces a shot.. Theory included then im all onboard..  this could go really well or be the final nail in the cinematic star wars verse (at least for disney)

author: @MeatloafMike1
text:
While it makes sense that they want to use AI to expedite the film making process to save money (because they keep spending way too much on terrible shows that have no hope of turning profit. ugh.), I cannot stomach the thought of supporting AI after what these companies have done to the memory and storage markets. I'm sick of it. 
Were you unsure about buying a Steam Deck and were waiting for a good opportunity? Too late. Now they're going to be perpetually out of stock and scalped on Ebay because of AI. 
Need to back-up your computer? Get ready to pay a shitload of cash for that HDD. 
This has to end.
